video_title: The New Plan to Make Ethereum Win
gist: Two ETH Labs co-founders (Onsgar and Casper), former Ethereum Foundation core researchers, unveil ETH Labs: a new nonprofit R&D arm funded by Bitmine, SharpLink and Joe Lubin, explicitly built to be 'pro-market, pro-growth, pro-ETH' and to push Ethereum into the role of the single global hub of internet finance while the EF retreats to a narrow 'crypto values' (censorship-resistance/privacy/security) mandate. The through-line: co-develop the protocol and the ETH asset together, aggressively scale L1 (targeting 3x/year), solve interoperability, and chase a trillion-dollar ETH.

themes.theme: ETH Labs mandate and division of labor with the Ethereum Foundation

insights.claim: ETH Labs is a new nonprofit R&D lab whose explicit mission is to 'make Ethereum and ETH win,' positioned as a second protocol-development center alongside the Ethereum Foundation.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: For the first time Ethereum has a well-funded second protocol steward with an unapologetically pro-asset, pro-growth mandate.
insights.quote: EALabs is a nonprofit um R&D lab and very simply put our mission really is just to make Ethereum and ETH win.

insights.claim: The Ethereum Foundation is deliberately narrowing its 2026 mandate to 'crypto values' (censorship resistance, open source, privacy, security) and reducing headcount and budget, leaving gaps like scaling, growth and adoption for other orgs to fill.
insights.speaker: Casper
insights.so_what: EF's retreat creates an explicit vacuum in scaling and adoption that ETH Labs is being spun up to occupy.
insights.quote: reducing its focus to some extent and really double down on crops. So censorship resistance, open source, privacy and security.

insights.claim: The split is framed as EF being the immutable 'Vatican/monastery' guarding Ethereum's soul while ETH Labs exports those properties to the world; the founders insist the roles are collaborative, not mutually exclusive.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Investors should read ETH Labs as complementary go-to-market for Ethereum rather than a governance rival.
insights.quote: I think I would maybe describe it a bit like a monastery like a like the Vatican of Ethereum.

insights.claim: ETH Labs and EF are described as sharing the same end vision but differing on path dependency: ETH Labs rejects the 'first perfect the tech, then bring finance on-chain' sequencing and insists adoption must happen in parallel with hardening.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: The real friction between the two orgs is prioritization and sequencing of hard-fork content, not values.
insights.quote: we can't just first get all the tech out of the way make sure that Ethereum is perfectly robust this postquantum secure ... it needs to happen in parallel

insights.claim: The founders explicitly reject the idea of 'running Ethereum like a company,' arguing that would fight over a smaller pie than Ethereum has already won; instead they want to double down on decentralization while embracing adoption.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Signals ETH Labs will preserve credible neutrality as a moat rather than centralize governance for speed.
insights.quote: the answer is also not to like just run Ethereum like a company ... then now we're fighting about like a pie that's much smaller than what Ethereum already has achieved

insights.claim: ETH Labs is not simply porting the founders' old EF workstreams; some prior workstreams will continue inside the EF or be collaborated on, while ETH Labs will drop others and take on new ones given a lean five-person starting team.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: The org boundary is fluid; expect overlapping ownership and joint research rather than a clean handoff.
insights.quote: We we're starting as a team of five. Um we have plans to hire beyond that but really stay ultimately very lean

themes.theme: Ethereum as the single hub of on-chain finance

insights.claim: The core thesis is that the global economy coming on-chain resolves to one of two outcomes: many independent chains linked by brittle, repeatedly-failing bridges, or one central node (Ethereum) that everything links back into with shared, trustless state.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: The bull case rests on Ethereum capturing a winner-take-most 'central settlement node' position that defaults become sticky.
insights.quote: bridges keep failing because ultimately bridges between independent chains can only ever be so secure ... these chains just do not have a shared state.

insights.claim: The founders assert there is 'no second best neutral chain' with a genuine shot at being the central hub of global finance, but stress the outcome is not inevitable and Ethereum could lose to the fragmented-chains world.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Frames Ethereum's neutrality as a unique, non-substitutable asset while explicitly denying inevitability.
insights.quote: There is no second best neutral chain. There's no second best chain that actually has a shot at this. But that doesn't mean it's inevitable.

insights.claim: Winning requires that launching a new chain on Ethereum becomes a 'no-brainer'; today it is not, because interop friction is too high and the upside/network effects of being an L2 are not clear or realized enough.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific competitive gap (interop UX and L2 value proposition) that must be closed to defend against rival L1s.
insights.quote: it needs to be a no-brainer that of course I do this in Ethereum ... and today it's not a no-brainer today the friction is also too high

insights.claim: In the 10-year success case, L2s are just an 'implementation detail' of zones; permissioned, app-specific and geographic zones proliferate but must be interconnected with instant, trustless transfers and cross-zone collateral reuse.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: The end-state vision is a unified liquidity/collateral fabric across many zones, not a single monolithic chain.
insights.quote: I keep saying zones because L2s is like the the way that works today. But in a way that's an implementation detail

insights.claim: Solving interoperability 'once and for all' is presented as a non-negotiable condition for Ethereum to be the special chain, cited as one of ETH Labs' most exciting workstreams.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Interop is elevated from a nice-to-have to an existential requirement for the central-hub thesis.
insights.quote: there is just no way that Ethereum will be the special chain if we do not solve intop once and for all

insights.claim: The upcoming Fusaka hard fork will deliver as much as 4x L1 throughput scaling, framed as the near-term scaling win ETH Labs is championing.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Concrete near-term catalyst: a 4x L1 capacity increase from the next hard fork.
insights.quote: there will be a huge um m scaling benefit coming out of that um as much as like 4x um kind of scaling throughut

insights.claim: Onsgar is pushing a target of roughly 3x L1 scaling per year on a permanent scaling trajectory, which he considers very achievable.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: A stated, quantified L1 scaling cadence gives investors a benchmark to track Ethereum's throughput growth.
insights.quote: I've been floating this this goal of like um 3x scaling per year. I think that's very um achievable.

insights.claim: ETH Labs is organizing internally into three pillars: 'Chain' (core protocol), 'Platform' (chain-to-application intersection like interop and block construction), and 'Growth' (adoption/application awareness), the last being newest to them.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Reveals ETH Labs' operating structure and that 'Growth' is a novel, application-driven function for former core researchers.
insights.quote: we internally we're structuring it is ... chain which is anything core protocol related and then platform and growth

insights.claim: Named priority workstreams beyond scaling include time-to-finality, time-to-inclusion, data scaling for L2s (blobs), and unlocking block-construction innovation ('clubm'/inclusion-list style designs).
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Maps the specific protocol levers ETH Labs intends to pull to improve UX and MEV/block-building markets.
insights.quote: there is um there's things like um time to finality time to inclusion there's things like data scaling for the L2s

insights.claim: L1 must scale enough to host the liquidity and cross-zone trade balancing directly, because at today's throughput levels the L1 is 'just not good enough' to serve as the settlement layer for global finance.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Positions aggressive L1 scaling (not just L2 offload) as essential to Ethereum capturing settlement value.
insights.quote: at at even at today's throughput levels that's just not good enough ... you need to be able to really have a lot of L1 available box space

themes.theme: ETH the asset, value accrual and the trillion-dollar target

insights.claim: ETH Labs' explicit position is that Ethereum and ETH can only win together and must be co-developed; treating the protocol as a purely technical project with ETH value accrual as an afterthought is called a mistake.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: A dedicated steward now explicitly weighs ETH value accrual when shaping protocol changes, a gap ETH holders long complained about.
insights.quote: I think sometimes the protocol is being developed really just as a as a purely technical project and the outcome ... the effects on ETH are a little bit of an afterthought and I think that's just a mistake

insights.claim: ETH Labs' internal north star is articulated as 'what does it take for ETH to become a trillion-dollar asset,' noting there are very few individual trillion-dollar assets on earth.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Sets an explicit, ambitious price/market-cap north star that frames the org's success metric for ETH holders.
insights.quote: what does it take for ETH you know to become a trillion dollar asset right like that's ... one of our north stars

insights.claim: Onsgar notes that excluding stablecoins only eight crypto assets exceed $10B market cap and only two (Bitcoin and ETH) exceed $100B, arguing the market is asking Ethereum 'what's round two' after it hit its current level five years ago.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Frames ETH's stalled market cap as evidence the market wants a fresh growth narrative, which ETH Labs aims to supply.
insights.quote: there's only eight um crypto assets above $10 billion market cap. Um that's not a lot. And there's only two above hundred billion um Bitcoin and ETH.

insights.claim: The value-accrual logic: an asset at Ethereum's core must be valuable enough to secure roughly a quadrillion dollars of eventual on-chain global assets (referencing Justin Drake's 'one quadrillion' talk), so ETH's current market cap is explicitly deemed insufficient.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Ties ETH's required valuation to the total value it must economically secure, implying large upside if adoption thesis holds.
insights.quote: if you roughly sum up all the assets in the entire world, you maybe get to like one quadrillion dollars and ... how do you basically secure one quadrillion dollars? Well ... you need an asset at the core.

insights.claim: The founders sequence the ETH thesis: first de-risk that Ethereum is 'inevitable' and here to stay (like Bitcoin's aura), then define ETH's specific value-accrual role within that world.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Value accrual is treated as a second-order problem gated on first establishing Ethereum's inevitability.
insights.quote: step one ... just d-risk Ethereum is here to stay ... And so then step two is once you've d-rised that part ... then you define the specific role of each within that.

insights.claim: ETH Labs' core donors are Bitmine, SharpLink and Joe Lubin, described as the two largest individual ETH holders plus Lubin, with over 50 additional ecosystem contributors including core devs and DeFi founders.
insights.speaker: Casper
insights.so_what: ETH Labs is bankrolled by the largest ETH treasury companies, aligning its funders' incentives directly with ETH price.
insights.quote: there's Bitmine, Sharpling, Joe Luben are sort of our um our core donors

insights.claim: ETH Labs states it has a solid two-to-three-year runway with no strings attached from Bitmine/SharpLink, and that any ongoing relationship with them (as partners, not funders) is kept separate from the donations to preserve neutrality.
insights.speaker: Casper
insights.so_what: Runway and no-strings framing are meant to reassure that ETH Labs won't be captured by its treasury-company backers.
insights.quote: we have like solid 2 three year runway um with kind of uh ambitious um hiring goals.

insights.claim: ETH Labs plans to return to the community in one year with a concrete track record to justify continued funding, deliberately using dependence on re-funding as a forcing function against typical nonprofit disconnection from real-world feedback.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Built-in annual accountability loop aligns ETH Labs with both ETH holders and builders and guards against nonprofit drift.
insights.quote: in a year we come back to the community and we're like hey this is our track record ... did that actually created enough value that you would want us to keep existing

insights.claim: ETH Labs is exploring a long-term sustainable funding model contingent on success, hinting at leveraging a 'big success case' in helping ETH into self-sustaining financing rather than perpetual donations.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Signals a possible future endowment/yield-style model tied to ETH performance, but no concrete mechanism yet.
insights.quote: how can we then maybe leverage that into more like long-term sustainable um funding

insights.claim: ETH Labs launches with five founding members (Onsgar, Casper, Barnaby, Josh Rudolf, Julian Ma) and reports hundreds of inbound applicants, intending to stay lean but hire world-class talent as workstreams are defined.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Concentrates significant Ethereum core-research talent density in one new org, a signal of where protocol influence is shifting.
insights.quote: We've had like hundreds of of of inbounds already

insights.claim: Onsgar frames the emergence of ETH Labs as going 'from n to n+1' orgs, arguing Ethereum's day-to-day prioritization has always been distributed across many contributors while the EF charts the long-term course.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Downplays governance-concentration fears by framing ETH Labs as continuity of Ethereum's already-decentralized development.
insights.quote: I don't think it's like going from one to two here. It's really just it's more going from you know from n to n plus one.

insights.claim: ETH Labs analogizes the EF/ETH Labs 'multi-node' future to Ethereum's client diversity: it raises coordination cost and makes shipping hard forks harder, but delivers resilience that is precisely what could let Ethereum reach global scale.
insights.speaker: Casper
insights.so_what: Reframes governance friction and slower hard forks as a deliberate resilience feature rather than a bug.
insights.quote: this sort of multi-node future is precisely kind of bringing this client diversity mentality to the ecosystem

insights.claim: ETH Labs has already begun direct consultation with finance and DeFi builders (e.g. Hayden Adams of Uniswap) asking concretely what would make their lives easier, signaling a demand-driven, market-responsive protocol approach.
insights.speaker: Onsgar
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence ETH Labs intends to feed application-builder needs back into protocol changes, a shift from EF's more neutral posture.
insights.quote: we've had um conversations with with um already Hayden from Uniswap. Just like very simple just like hey, what what concretely right now would make your life easier

insights.claim: Host David Hoffman argues ETH is currently cheap and near a good entry point, invoking that ETH previously peaked near $600B market cap (within striking distance of a trillion), used as a proof-of-concept that a trillion-dollar ETH is imaginable.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: A prior ~$600B ETH peak is cited as evidence the trillion-dollar target is within reach, framing current prices as a discount.
insights.quote: At its peak, if Ether was at 600 billion, which is somewhat within striking distance of a trillion dollar asset.
